questions.text: In the context of auditor responsibilities, what is the precise definition of 'fraud' according to the ACFE's fraud triangle concept?
options: A. Any intentional misstatement of financial records regardless of motivation or opportunity
B. An intentional deception or misrepresentation made by an individual knowing it is false, with the intent to deprive another of a right or to cause loss
C. Any error discovered during financial statement audit procedures
D. Unintentional mistakes in accounting entries that result in material misstatements

questions.text: Which of the following best defines 'professional skepticism' as required in auditor fraud detection responsibilities?
options: A. An attitude of distrust toward all management representations without exception
B. An attitude that includes a questioning mind and a critical assessment of audit evidence, recognizing the possibility of fraud
C. A presumption that all transactions are fraudulent until proven otherwise
D. The auditor's legal obligation to report all suspected fraud to law enforcement

questions.text: According to fraud detection standards, what is the primary distinction between 'misstatements' and 'irregularities' in financial reporting?
options: A. Misstatements are intentional while irregularities are accidental
B. Irregularities are intentional misstatements or omissions of amounts or disclosures, whereas misstatements can be either intentional or unintentional
C. Misstatements have no materiality threshold while irregularities must be material
D. Irregularities only apply to revenue transactions while misstatements apply to all accounts

questions.text: In the context of auditor responsibilities, what does 'management's assessment of fraud risk' specifically refer to?
options: A. The external auditor's independent evaluation of fraud exposure in the organization
B. Management's process of identifying, analyzing, and responding to fraud risks as part of internal control assessment
C. The audit committee's quarterly review of suspected fraudulent transactions
D. The CFE's forensic investigation findings regarding actual fraud incidents

questions.text: What is the precise definition of 'the auditor's responsibility' regarding detection of fraud under modern auditing standards?
options: A. To guarantee absolute detection of all fraud occurring in an organization
B. To provide reasonable assurance of detecting material fraud, recognizing inherent limitations of audit procedures
C. To investigate all unusual transactions regardless of materiality
D. To perform continuous monitoring throughout the fiscal year

questions.text: According to fraud examination standards, what is meant by the term 'fraud risk assessment' in the planning phase?
options: A. Identifying specific fraudsters within the organization based on their prior criminal history
B. The systematic process of evaluating the organization's vulnerability to fraud and the likelihood of occurrence
C. Determining which accounts will definitely contain fraudulent entries
D. Assigning fraud investigation personnel to monitor all employee communications

questions.text: What is the technical definition of 'the auditor's detective responsibility' versus 'the auditor's preventive responsibility'?
options: A. Detective responsibility involves discovering existing fraud; preventive responsibility involves the auditor implementing control procedures
B. Both responsibilities are identical and interchangeable terms
C. Detective responsibility is a CFE function only; preventive responsibility is a CPA function only
D. Preventive responsibility requires the auditor to prosecute fraudsters while detective responsibility involves investigation

questions.text: In audit terminology, what does 'brainstorming about fraud risks' specifically encompass in the planning process?
options: A. A casual discussion among audit team members without documentation
B. A structured, documented discussion where the audit team considers how and where fraud could occur in the organization
C. An accusatory meeting where management is confronted with suspected fraud
D. A confidential session where only the external auditor participates without internal audit involvement

questions.text: According to auditing standards, what is the precise definition of 'management override of controls' in the context of auditor fraud responsibilities?
options: A. The audit committee's authority to modify internal control procedures
B. The CFE's ability to bypass normal audit procedures during investigation
C. Management's intentional circumvention of internal controls to achieve a desired outcome, often for fraud purposes
D. The external auditor's right to override management decisions during the audit process

questions.text: What is the key distinction between 'occupational fraud' and 'financial statement fraud' as defined in fraud audit standards?
options: A. Occupational fraud involves external parties; financial statement fraud involves only internal parties
B. Occupational fraud targets the organization's assets directly; financial statement fraud targets the accuracy of financial reports
C. Financial statement fraud is less serious than occupational fraud
D. Occupational fraud is always material while financial statement fraud may be immaterial

questions.text: In the context of auditor responsibilities, what does the term 'red flags' or 'fraud risk indicators' specifically mean?
options: A. Confirmed instances of fraud that have been prosecuted
B. Warning signs or conditions that suggest a higher risk of fraud occurring in a particular transaction or account
C. Financial ratios that are outside acceptable ranges
D. Any variance between budgeted and actual figures

questions.text: According to auditing standards, what is the precise meaning of 'the auditor's obligation to consider the risk of material misstatement due to fraud'?
options: A. The auditor must assume all transactions are fraudulent
B. The auditor must incorporate specific fraud risk factors into risk assessment and design audit procedures responsive to identified fraud risks
C. The auditor is only responsible for considering fraud in high-risk accounts
D. The auditor's obligation ends once initial audit procedures are completed

questions.text: What is the technical definition of 'fraud awareness' in the context of auditor responsibilities and training?
options: A. A one-time orientation about fraud definitions provided to new audit staff
B. The ongoing understanding and recognition of fraud schemes, risk factors, and detection techniques by audit personnel
C. The auditor's personal experience with committing fraud
D. Management's knowledge of fraud prevention policies only

questions.text: When distinguishing between fraud detection and fraud prevention responsibilities, which statement most accurately reflects the auditor's obligation under professional standards?
options: A. Auditors bear equal responsibility for both prevention and detection of all organizational fraud
B. Auditors are primarily responsible for detection, while management bears primary responsibility for prevention through effective controls
C. Internal auditors focus exclusively on prevention, whereas external auditors focus exclusively on detection
D. Both auditors and management share identical fraud prevention and detection responsibilities under COSO framework

questions.text: How does the forensic auditor's approach to evidence collection differ fundamentally from the external auditor's approach when fraud is suspected?
options: A. Forensic auditors focus on statistical sampling, while external auditors perform detailed transaction testing
B. Forensic auditors prioritize chain of custody and admissibility in legal proceedings, while external auditors emphasize sufficiency for financial reporting conclusions
C. External auditors have greater authority to subpoena evidence than forensic auditors
D. Forensic auditors are restricted to only examining digital evidence, unlike external auditors

questions.text: Under the ACFE's Standards for Internal Investigations, what distinguishes a CFE's investigative scope from a compliance officer's scope when responding to a fraud allegation?
options: A. Compliance officers investigate regulatory violations exclusively, while CFEs investigate all suspected fraud regardless of regulatory implications
B. CFEs may interview subjects without advance notice, while compliance officers must provide formal notice procedures
C. Compliance officers report only to external regulators, while CFEs report to audit committees
D. CFEs have authority to make termination decisions, while compliance officers cannot

questions.text: Which distinction is critical when evaluating whether a transaction represents fraudulent financial reporting versus misappropriation of assets?
options: A. Fraudulent financial reporting always involves larger dollar amounts than asset misappropriation
B. Fraudulent financial reporting requires falsifying accounting records to deceive users, while misappropriation typically conceals unauthorized asset removal
C. Misappropriation is always perpetrated by lower-level employees, while fraudulent reporting is perpetrated by senior management
D. Fraudulent financial reporting requires external accomplices, while misappropriation can only be perpetrated internally

questions.text: When an auditor identifies a red flag indicating possible management override, what action differentiates appropriate professional skepticism from unwarranted suspicion?
options: A. Immediately reporting management to law enforcement based on the red flag alone
B. Extending audit procedures and seeking corroborating evidence before modifying the audit conclusion
C. Reducing audit procedures in high-risk areas to avoid offending management
D. Treating all red flags with equal weight regardless of the control environment context

questions.text: How should an auditor differentiate between a control deficiency and a control that has been overridden when assessing management's control environment?
options: A. Control deficiencies are design flaws, while overrides represent intentional circumvention despite adequate design
B. Overrides only occur in IT systems, while deficiencies occur in manual processes
C. Control deficiencies indicate management integrity issues, while overrides indicate only design problems
D. Both terms are interchangeable and represent the same control weakness

questions.text: What is the fundamental difference between a fraud scheme characterized as 'lapping' and one characterized as 'skimming'?
options: A. Lapping involves cash concealment, while skimming involves check manipulation
B. Lapping uses subsequent receipts to cover prior theft while maintaining records, whereas skimming removes cash before recording
C. Lapping requires collusion, while skimming can be perpetrated by a single individual
D. Skimming always involves sales transactions, while lapping always involves payroll transactions

questions.text: In evaluating journal entry controls, how should an auditor distinguish between a system that prevents unauthorized entries and one that merely detects them after the fact?
options: A. Both provide equivalent levels of fraud risk mitigation if detected promptly
B. Preventive controls reduce the risk of unauthorized entries occurring, while detective controls identify entries already made, creating a timing gap for potential fraud
C. Detective controls are always more cost-effective than preventive controls
D. Preventive controls can only be implemented in automated systems

questions.text: How does the auditor's documentation standard differ when a fraud is confirmed versus when fraud is merely suspected but unconfirmed?
options: A. Suspected fraud requires no documentation if it is subsequently disproven

B. Confirmed fraud requires detailed documentation of investigation findings, while suspected fraud requires documentation of the red flags, procedures performed, and conclusions even if fraud is not substantiated
C. Suspected fraud must be immediately reported to law enforcement documentation
D. Both confirmed and suspected fraud are documented identically without distinction

questions.text: When comparing the auditor's response to fraud detected in a public versus private company context, which regulatory distinction most significantly affects the auditor's reporting obligations?
options: A. Public companies require audit committee notification, while private companies do not

B. Auditors of public companies under SOX have specific requirements regarding audit committee communication timelines and content, whereas private company auditors must follow AICPA standards with less prescriptive timelines
C. Private company auditors are prohibited from communicating fraud to management
D. Public company auditors must report all suspected fraud to the SEC immediately

questions.text: How should an auditor distinguish between a material weakness in internal controls and a significant deficiency when assessing fraud risk?
options: A. Material weaknesses involve fraud while significant deficiencies do not

B. A material weakness is a deficiency (or combination) where likelihood of undetected misstatement is reasonably possible, while significant deficiency is less than reasonably possible but still important to governance
C. Significant deficiencies are always easier and less costly to remediate than material weaknesses
D. Material weaknesses require immediate termination of management, while significant deficiencies do not

questions.text: In distinguishing between occupational fraud schemes, what characteristic specifically differentiates corruption schemes from both financial statement fraud and asset misappropriation?
options: A. Corruption always involves executives, while other schemes involve employees
B. Corruption involves the use of one's position to gain improper advantage through dealing with third parties, distinct from direct asset theft or accounting falsification
C. Corruption schemes are always discovered within one year, unlike other fraud schemes
D. Corruption cannot occur in public sector organizations

questions.text: What critical distinction should an auditor recognize when evaluating whether questionable transactions represent possible fraud versus aggressive but legitimate accounting interpretations?
options: A. Fraud always involves intentional deception or concealment, while aggressive accounting may be based on differing but defensible technical interpretations
B. Only transactions exceeding materiality thresholds can constitute fraud
C. Auditors cannot distinguish between fraud and aggressive accounting under any circumstances
D. Legitimate accounting interpretations require no documentation, while fraudulent transactions require written authorization

questions.text: During a fraud investigation, an auditor discovers that a sales manager has been creating fictitious customer accounts to inflate revenue. The auditor determines this represents a material misstatement. Which of the following best describes the auditor's primary responsibility at this stage?
options: A. Immediately report the fraud to law enforcement without notifying management
B. Assess the impact on the financial statements and determine appropriate reporting to those charged with governance
C. Continue the audit as planned and document findings only in internal audit files
D. Require the employee to reimburse the company before any reporting occurs

questions.text: A CFE is designing audit procedures to detect potential inventory fraud at a distribution center. Which procedure would be MOST effective in identifying collusion between warehouse staff and external parties?
options: A. Performing standard physical inventory counts at year-end
B. Analyzing inventory movement patterns and comparing them to sales data for anomalies
C. Reviewing only the accounts payable invoices for the inventory period
D. Verifying that all inventory items have proper documentation tags

questions.text: An auditor suspects that a finance director may be manipulating expense reimbursement records by submitting duplicate claims. What is the MOST appropriate first step in gathering preliminary evidence?
options: A. Conduct a surprise audit of the director's office and personal files
B. Review expense reimbursement reports and compare them to supporting documentation for duplicates or inconsistencies
C. Interview subordinates without the director's knowledge to gather informal allegations
D. Immediately escalate the suspicion to the CFO as a matter of urgency

questions.text: Which of the following scenarios would BEST indicate that a company's internal controls are vulnerable to fraud perpetrated by someone in a position of financial authority?
options: A. The accounting department has a policy requiring employees to take mandatory annual vacations
B. The accounts payable clerk processes all vendor payments without supervisory review or approval
C. Journal entries are recorded in a centralized accounting system with audit trails enabled
D. All bank reconciliations are performed by the accounting manager and reviewed by the controller

questions.text: A CFE is evaluating the segregation of duties within a procurement process. The same employee is responsible for requisitioning inventory, approving purchases, receiving goods, and processing payments. Which type of fraud is this control weakness MOST likely to enable?
options: A. Kickback schemes involving vendors and purchasing employees
B. Unauthorized disclosure of company trade secrets
C. Embezzlement through false payroll entries
D. Insider trading based on material non-public information

questions.text: During a financial statement audit, an auditor notices that several large transactions near year-end lack proper supporting documentation. Management attributes this to time constraints and promises to provide documentation later. What should the auditor do?
options: A. Accept management's explanation and issue an unqualified opinion based on subsequent documentation provided
B. Insist on obtaining adequate evidence before concluding the audit; consider whether fraud indicators are present
C. Report the missing documentation to the external auditor only, not to the audit committee
D. Reduce audit scope in this area to compensate for the timing issue

questions.text: A company has implemented a new expense management system that requires all employees to submit digital receipts for reimbursement. An auditor wants to assess whether this control effectively prevents duplicate reimbursements. Which analytical procedure would be MOST appropriate?
options: A. Test a random sample of 50 reimbursement transactions for proper approval signatures
B. Use data analytics to identify patterns of repeated receipt submissions from the same employee on the same date
C. Interview the system administrator about the technical capabilities of the software
D. Review the system's user access logs to confirm who has input permissions

questions.text: An internal auditor discovers that the company's CFO has been authorizing expense reimbursements for personal items by classifying them as business expenses. The CFO is the auditor's direct supervisor. What is the MOST appropriate action for the auditor to take?
options: A. Remain silent to avoid jeopardizing the employment relationship with the CFO
B. Discuss the matter informally with the CFO first to give them an opportunity to correct the error
C. Report the finding through the appropriate reporting channel, which may include the audit committee or ethics hotline, independent of the CFO
D. Document the finding only in personal files and wait to see if it recurs before taking action

questions.text: A CFE is investigating suspected asset misappropriation in a retail environment. The perpetrator is suspected to be a store manager with 15 years of tenure and no previous disciplinary issues. Which behavioral or situational factor is MOST relevant in this fraud scenario?
options: A. The perpetrator's lack of formal accounting education prevents detection of their scheme
B. Long tenure and trust may have created an opportunity because controls were relaxed or bypassed
C. The absence of previous discipline guarantees the person would not commit fraud
D. Retail environments are inherently resistant to fraud due to the nature of inventory controls

questions.text: An auditor performing substantive procedures discovers that a company has capitalized maintenance expenses as fixed assets in violation of accounting standards. The amount is material. Which element of the fraud triangle is MOST directly indicated by this finding?
options: A. Pressure, because the company may be trying to meet earnings targets
B. Opportunity, because the accounting system does not prevent capitalization of maintenance costs
C. Rationalization, because management believes the expenses extend asset useful life
D. All three elements are equally indicated by this finding

questions.text: A CFE is planning a surprise audit of petty cash disbursements to detect potential fraud. The petty cash custodian has been with the company for 10 years and manages approximately 200 transactions per month. What sampling approach would BEST detect a systematic pattern of petty cash misappropriation?
options: A. Test all transactions for the most recent month only to minimize disruption
B. Use systematic sampling across a 12-month period to identify recurring patterns and inconsistencies
C. Rely on management's representation that no fraud has occurred and test only transactions exceeding $100
D. Test only the largest transactions, as small thefts are immaterial to the financial statements

questions.text: During a fraud investigation, an auditor obtains email communications showing that the purchasing manager and a vendor representative discussed 'special arrangements' regarding pricing. However, the vendor invoices to the company reflect standard market prices. What is the MOST appropriate next step?
options: A. Conclude that no fraud occurred because the invoices are at market rates
B. Continue investigating to determine the nature of the 'special arrangements' and whether kickbacks or other undisclosed payments are occurring
C. Report the email exchange immediately to law enforcement as evidence of conspiracy
D. Restrict access to these emails to prevent contamination of evidence in case litigation ensues

questions.text: An auditor is reviewing the company's conflict-of-interest policy and notes that it requires employees to disclose outside business interests annually. The policy, however, lacks a mechanism for ongoing monitoring or verification of disclosures. Which statement BEST describes the control effectiveness?
options: A. The policy is highly effective because it demonstrates management's commitment to fraud prevention
B. The policy has a significant weakness because without monitoring and verification, undisclosed conflicts may go undetected
C. The policy eliminates the need for auditor procedures related to conflict-of-interest identification
D. Annual disclosures are sufficient control because employees understand the serious legal consequences of non-disclosure

questions.text: During a fraud risk assessment, an auditor identifies that a company's procurement department lacks segregation of duties between purchase authorization and payment approval. The controller argues that the company's IT system provides compensating controls through automated exception reports reviewed monthly. How should the auditor evaluate this control environment?
options: A. Accept the compensating controls as sufficient if exception reports are comprehensive and reviewed timely
B. Conclude that manual compensating controls cannot adequately offset the absence of segregation of duties in a high-risk transaction cycle
C. Recommend that the company implement detective controls in addition to the existing preventive controls before finalizing the assessment
D. Determine that compensating controls are effective only if they operate in real-time and restrict transaction processing automatically

questions.text: A CFE discovers that a sales manager has been recording fictitious revenue by creating shell customers in the system. The fraudster modified the customer master file directly, bypassing the normal change request workflow. Which audit procedure would have been most effective in detecting this fraud earlier?
options: A. Reviewing sales journal entries for unusual account combinations or round-dollar amounts
B. Testing the effectiveness of access controls over master file changes and comparing recorded customer data against independent sources
C. Analyzing the aging of accounts receivable to identify customers with unusual payment patterns
D. Examining sales contracts and shipping documents for a sample of large transactions
questions.tag: Fraud Detection Procedures and Audit Evidence

questions.text: An external auditor is planning the audit of a manufacturing company with a history of inventory theft in its warehouse. The finance director has implemented RFID tracking and real-time inventory monitoring. However, the CFO mentions that warehouse personnel can override the system with supervisor approval. What is the auditor's primary concern regarding this control environment?
options: A. The RFID technology is outdated and should be replaced with blockchain technology
B. Management has created a potential opportunity for collusion and fraud by building override capabilities into a key preventive control
C. The real-time monitoring system is too costly relative to the value of inventory at risk
D. The auditor should focus only on the supervisory approval process and not question the override capability

questions.text: During substantive testing, an auditor notices that a company's accounts payable department processed duplicate invoices from vendors on two separate occasions within six months. When investigated, the CFE learns that both incidents involved the same vendor and occurred when different employees processed the payments. What does this pattern suggest about the company's fraud risk?
options: A. The company is experiencing accidental process failures rather than intentional fraud
B. A single vendor is systematically committing fraud and should be immediately blacklisted
C. The control environment lacks preventive controls to detect duplicate invoices, creating vulnerability to both accidental errors and intentional fraud
D. The audit procedures were ineffective and should be redesigned to focus on vendor relationships
questions.tag: Fraud Risk Indicators and Pattern Recognition

questions.text: A publicly traded company's board of directors asks the external auditor whether a proposed internal audit charter should grant the internal audit function direct reporting lines to both the audit committee and the CFO. The external auditor is concerned about audit independence. What is the most appropriate recommendation?
options: A. Internal audit should report exclusively to the CFO to maintain operational efficiency and reduce bureaucratic delays
B. Internal audit should have a primary reporting line to the audit committee with no operational reporting to management
C. The dual reporting structure is acceptable if the audit committee chair and CFO have a strong professional relationship
D. Reporting to the CFO is necessary for internal audit to obtain the cooperation needed to execute its plans effectively

questions.text: During a fraud investigation, a CFE discovers that the company's HR director altered employee timecards to reduce the hours worked by a favored employee, creating a false record of productivity. The CFE must now determine the appropriate scope of the investigation. Which factor is most critical in defining whether this is an isolated incident or evidence of a systemic control deficiency?
options: A. Whether the employee is still employed by the company
B. Whether similar alterations have occurred with other employees and whether the system permits such modifications without adequate segregation of duties
C. Whether the amount of money involved exceeds materiality thresholds
D. Whether the HR director acted alone or with assistance from the payroll department

questions.text: An auditor is evaluating a company's journal entry control procedures. The company's accounting policy requires that all manual journal entries above $50,000 be approved by the controller before posting. During testing, the auditor discovers that the controller approved an entry for $49,500 that contained several related adjustments that, when combined, totaled $125,000 across multiple accounts. What does this finding indicate about the control environment?
options: A. The control threshold is appropriately set and has functioned as designed
B. The company has implemented a strong compensating control by requiring controller approval for all entries
C. Management has potentially structured transactions to circumvent the approval control, suggesting a need for enhanced detective procedures
D. The auditor should increase the materiality threshold to align with the company's risk tolerance
questions.tag: Management Fraud Techniques and Control Circumvention

questions.text: A CFE is retained to investigate suspected embezzlement in the finance department. The CFE determines that three employees had access to the system during the time period when unauthorized transactions occurred. Management pressures the CFE to quickly identify the perpetrator to minimize reputational damage. How should the CFE respond to this pressure?
options: A. Accelerate the investigation timeline by making preliminary accusations based on preliminary evidence to satisfy management's timeline
B. Maintain investigative independence and thoroughness, documenting all evidence according to professional standards, regardless of pressure
C. Negotiate with management to reduce the scope of the investigation in exchange for a faster conclusion
D. Focus only on the employee with the longest tenure, as experience suggests they are statistically more likely to commit fraud
questions.tag: CFE Professional Standards and Investigative Ethics

questions.text: An external auditor is assessing fraud risks at a technology company with a decentralized structure where divisional controllers have significant autonomy over their financial reporting. The auditor notes that divisional financial statements are not consolidated or reviewed by corporate accounting until after the quarterly earnings announcement. What is the primary fraud risk this structure creates?
options: A. The divisional controllers might face excessive time pressure that leads to unintentional errors
B. Opportunities for divisional management override of controls and delayed detection increase because corporate-level review occurs after public disclosure
C. The external audit firm will lack sufficient resources to audit all divisions thoroughly
D. The company's IT systems are likely outdated and require modernization before effective controls can be implemented
questions.tag: Organizational Structure and Fraud Opportunity Assessment

questions.text: During the planning phase of a financial statement audit, the auditor learns that the company recently terminated its internal audit function due to budget cuts and has reassigned internal audit responsibilities to the external audit team. The audit committee chair states this approach will reduce costs. What is the auditor's primary concern regarding this arrangement?
options: A. The external auditor's ability to maintain audit independence may be compromised by performing internal audit functions
B. The company will have no one to monitor the external auditor's work quality
C. The costs savings achieved will be offset by increased audit risk and reduced detection of management override
D. External auditors are more qualified than internal auditors to detect fraud and this is an improvement
questions.tag: External and Internal Audit Function Coordination

questions.text: A CFE is analyzing expense reimbursement fraud and identifies a pattern where an employee submitted multiple business meal expenses just below the company's $75 approval threshold, with similar vendors and timing each month. When the CFE aggregates these expenses, they total approximately $6,000 annually. What is the significance of this pattern in fraud risk assessment?
options: A. The total amount is immaterial and does not warrant further investigation
B. This pattern suggests intentional circumvention of controls through structuring, indicating a calculated fraud scheme rather than accidental non-compliance
C. The expenses are legitimate business meals and should be approved as submitted to maintain employee morale
D. The auditor should focus on larger expenses rather than wasting resources on routine meal reimbursements
questions.tag: Fraud Detection Through Data Analytics and Trend Analysis

questions.text: An auditor discovers that the company's accounts receivable aging report shows an unusually large number of accounts with zero aging days (current period only) despite the company's typical 60-day payment terms. Investigation reveals that a credit manager has been manually adjusting the transaction dates in the system to keep problematic accounts appearing current. Which control deficiency enabled this fraud?
options: A. Lack of segregation of duties between credit approval and system access for transaction date modifications
B. Absence of IT general controls restricting transaction date changes after initial posting
C. Failure to implement analytical procedures to monitor aging report trends period to period
D. All of the above represent control deficiencies that contributed to this fraud
questions.tag: Multiple Control Deficiencies and Layered Vulnerabilities

questions.text: During fraud risk assessment, an auditor evaluates management's incentive structure and discovers that the CEO's annual bonus is tied 70% to achieving revenue targets and 30% to expense reduction. The company is currently 5% below target with two weeks remaining in the quarter. What is the auditor's primary concern regarding fraud risk?
options: A. The incentive structure is well-balanced and motivates appropriate behavior
B. Management has significant financial incentive and motivation to misstate financial results, creating elevated fraud risk that requires enhanced audit procedures
C. The auditor should focus only on expense transactions since the expense reduction component is smaller
D. Revenue targets are industry-standard and do not create fraud risk
questions.tag: Incentive Structures and Fraud Motivation Analysis
